title: Odysseus and the Cyclops
description: Odysseus blinds the Cyclops Polyphemus and escapes from his cave by a clever trick.

latin_text.text: Ulixēs comitēsque suī in antrum Cyclōpis ingressī erant. Polyphēmus, ingēns
mōnstrum, Ulixem rogāvit quis esset. 'Nēmō' inquit Ulixēs 'mihi nōmen est.'
Polyphēmus, vīnō sōpītus, dormiēbat. Ulixēs, hāc occāsiōne ūsus, pālum
igneum in oculum mōnstrī impulit. Polyphēmus clāmāvit ut alii Cyclōpēs
adiuvārent. 'quis tē laedit?' rogāvērunt. 'Nēmō mē laedit!' respondit Polyphēmus.
itaque Cyclōpēs abiērunt putantēs nūllum periculum adesse.
postrīdiē Ulixēs suōs sub ovibus celāvit. Polyphēmo ovēs ēmittente, Graecī

vocabulary_notes.meaning: overcome, sent to sleep (participle of sōpiō)
that no danger was present (indirect statement)
as he let out / sending out (participle of ēmittō)

model_translation.text: Ulysses and his companions had entered the cave of the Cyclops. Polyphemus, the huge monster, asked Ulysses who he was. 'Nobody is my name,' said Ulysses. Polyphemus, overcome by wine, was sleeping. Ulysses, making use of this opportunity, drove a burning stake into the eye of the monster.

Polyphemus shouted so that the other Cyclopes would help him. 'Who is harming you?' they asked. 'Nobody is harming me!' replied Polyphemus. And so the Cyclopes left, thinking that no danger was present. The next day Ulysses hid his men under the sheep. As Polyphemus let out the sheep, the Greeks escaped. Ulysses rejoicing boarded the ship.

key_phrases: had entered the cave of the Cyclops
drove a burning stake into the eye of the monster
shouted so that the other Cyclopes would help him
thinking that no danger was present
hid his men under the sheep
As Polyphemus let out the sheep, the Greeks escaped
